articles.title: Karur Vysya Bank shares jump 13% after Q1 profit hits record ₹756 crore
articles.excerpt:
Karur Vysya Bank shares jumped 9.6 per cent in early trade to ₹329.90 on the NSE after the bank reported its highest quarterly profit of ₹756 crore for the quarter ended June 2026 (Q1FY27), up 45 per cent on a y-o-y basis. The stock’s gains came despite the bank maintaining a cautious and moderate growth outlook for FY26-27.

The management cautioned that the strong Q1 growth should not be considered an indicator of full-year growth at the same pace, as business growth was front-loaded in Q1 and potentially Q2.

Karur Vysya Bank maintained its credit growth guidance at 1 per cent to 2 per cent over industry growth for the rest of the quarters.

The bank’s full-year Net Interest Margin (NIM)

articles.title: Top Gainers & Losers on July 21: Karur Vysya Bank, TVS Motor, Zen Tech, Meesho, Thermax among top gainers today
articles.excerpt:
The Indian stock market remained range-bound in Tuesday's trade (21 July), as investor sentiment stayed fragile amid escalating tensions in West Asia, while the first batch of June quarter earnings delivered a mixed performance, limiting investors' appetite for equities.

Despite the weak global cues, the benchmark indices ended with modest losses, supported by buying in select financial and auto stocks. The Nifty 50 eventually closed 0.21% lower at 24,186, while the S&P BSE Sensex ended the session 0.30% down at 77,480.

The broader markets, however, outperformed the benchmark indices, with both the Nifty Midcap 100 and Nifty Smallcap 100 ending the session with gains of up to 0.53%.

articles.title: Karur Vysya Bank shares soar 11% after stellar Q1 results. What investors should know
articles.excerpt:
Karur Vysya Bank shares rose sharply after the private lender posted a 45% year-on-year jump in Q1 net profit to Rs 756 crore. Strong growth in net interest income, an improved net interest margin, healthy loan and deposit growth, and stable asset quality lifted investor sentiment.

articles.title: Global Market: Private capital crucial to Europe's defence push as funding bottlenecks persist
articles.excerpt:
Private investment will be crucial to funding Europe's growing defence industry as government budgets remain constrained, finance executives said at the Farnborough International Airshow. They stressed that public spending alone is insufficient and called for regulatory reforms.

articles.title: West Asia conflict fuels defence stocks. Now comes the harder test
articles.excerpt:
This is a Mint Premium article gifted to you.Subscribe to enjoy similar stories.

The rally in defence stocks has shown little sign of losing steam. Having outperformed every year since the pandemic, the sector has extended its winning streak into 2026, with the Nifty Defence index up nearly 20% year to date against an 8% decline in the Nifty 50.

Renewed conflict in West Asia has added fresh fuel to the rally, reviving optimism around defence stocks and India's push for self-reliance. The question now is what drives the next leg: more orders or better execution?

articles.title: HAL, BEL to Bharat Dynamics: Defence stocks dip after escalation in the US-Iran war
articles.excerpt:
US-Iran war: Defence stocks like Hindustan Aeronuatics (HAL), Bharat Electronics Limited (BEL) and Bharat Dynamics fell in Monday's trading session after escalation in the US-Iran war.

HAL shares declined over 0.58% to ₹4,474 apiece, while shares of BEL and Bharat Dynamics dropped 0.78% and 0.55%, respectively, during Monday's trading session.

The defence stock witnessed a setback after Iran said the ceasefire with the US had effectively broken down, heightening concerns over potential disruptions to energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz.

articles.title: Mapping the Market: Nasdaq troubles run deeper that they appear
articles.excerpt:
NEW YORK, July 21 (Reuters) - The Nasdaq Composite has looked shaky since notching a record close on June 2, and a closer look at market internals suggests the weakness runs deeper than the headline index implies. Click here for a more detailed chart. The Nasdaq New High/New Low (NH/NL) Index, a popular gauge of how many stocks are hitting fresh highs versus lows, ended Monday at 40.6%, its weakest reading since April 10. This isn't a sudden shift — the warning signs have been building for months.

After the Nasdaq bottomed on March 30, the NH/NL Index rallied into late April. But when the Composite pushed on to new record highs, the breadth measure couldn't keep pace, posting a lower high i
articles.citation: Mapping the Market: Nasdaq troubles run deeper that they appear — Mint Markets, 2026-07-21. https://www.livemint.com/market/mapping-the-market-nasdaq-troubles-run-deeper-that-they-appear-11784628120361.html

articles.title: Euro zone bond yields tick higher as oil remains elevated; eyes on ECB
articles.excerpt:
Euro zone government bond yields are rising as oil prices remain elevated. This situation threatens inflation outlook and supports tighter European Central Bank policy. Investors are also watching Britain's gilt market after new Prime Minister Andy Burnham's appointment. Germany's 10-year bond yield reached its highest level in eight weeks. The European Central Bank is expected to hold its deposit rate steady this week.

articles.title: Japan bond jitters overshadow Takaichi's first economic policy roadmap
articles.excerpt:
Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's economic blueprint promises increased investment in growth areas. Market concerns persist regarding potential government interference in monetary policy decisions. Bond yields have risen, reflecting worries about Japan's substantial national debt. The administration adjusted its blueprint language to clarify the Bank of Japan's policy-setting independence. This focus on spending and low rates raises fears about Japan's financial stability.

articles.title: Global Market: Euro zone banks tighten credit standards amid geopolitical risks, ECB survey shows
articles.excerpt:
Euro zone banks tightened lending standards in the second quarter as geopolitical uncertainty and economic risks prompted greater caution, according to the ECB's latest Bank Lending Survey. While demand for business loans improved, lenders became more selective and expect credit conditions to tighten further in the coming months, reflecting persistent concerns over inflation, energy prices and economic growth.

articles.title: July 2026 euro area bank lending survey
articles.excerpt: According to the July 2026 bank lending survey (BLS), euro area banks reported a moderate net tightening of credit standards – banks’ internal guidelines or loan approval criteria – for loans or credit lines to enterprises in the second quarter of 2026 (net 7% of banks; Chart 1). Banks also reported a net tightening of credit standards for both loans to households for house purchase and consumer credit and other lending to households (net 9% and 12% respectively). For firms, the net tightening was lower than banks’ expectations in the previous round (19%). Perceived risks to the economic outlook and banks’ lower risk tolerance remained the main factors contributing to the tightening, as bank
articles.citation: July 2026 euro area bank lending survey — ECB press, 2026-07-21. https://www.ecb.europa.eu//press/pr/date/2026/html/ecb.pr260721~44ee50f75c.en.html

articles.title: HDFC Bank shares fall over 7 per cent in two days after Q1 earnings
articles.excerpt:
HDFC Bank shares fell more than 7 per cent over two trading sessions, erasing nearly ₹89,636 crore in market capitalisation, after investors reacted negatively to weaker-than-expected net interest margins in the June quarter

Shares of HDFC Bank extended their previous session’s decline, falling over 7 per cent in two days and wiping out Rs 89,636 crore from its market valuation, after the lender’s quarterly earnings disappointed on the margin front.

The stock declined 2.08 per cent to settle at Rs 761.45 on the BSE. During the day, it slipped 2.25 per cent to Rs 760.10.

At the NSE, the stock dipped 2.08 per cent to end at Rs 761.45.

Shares of HDFC Bank had tumbled over 5 per cent on Mond

articles.title: Indian rupee gains as oil comes off; inflows, RBI intervention in focus
articles.excerpt:
The Indian rupee experienced a slight upturn on Tuesday, buoyed by the prospect of improved diplomatic relations between the U.S. and Iran, which reduced oil market tensions. With strong capital inflows enhancing the foreign exchange reserves, the Reserve Bank of India reported mobilization of over twenty billion dollars. Investors are now keeping a close eye on the central bank's reserve management approach.

articles.title: India Keeps Buying Russian Oil at Near-Record Pace Despite Expired Waiver
articles.excerpt:
India Keeps Buying Russian Oil at Near-Record Pace Despite Expired Waiver

What I Cover Tsvetana Paraskova is an energy and commodities journalist who has contributed to Oilprice.com for nearly a decade, covering global energy markets, commodities,…

India's crude oil imports have remained close to record-high levels in July despite the end of the U.S. waiver the previous month.

The U.S. quietly let the waiver allowing the purchase of Russian oil loaded on tankers expire on June 17, just as the U.S. and Iran signed the memorandum of understanding to continue negotiations on a deal.

articles.title: Rupee gains as oil comes off; inflows, RBI intervention in focus
articles.excerpt:
The Indian rupee firmed slightly on Tuesday as hopes ​of diplomacy between the US and Iran pulled the ‌brakes on an oil rally that had ​driven the currency to a two-month low.

Traders ⁠are also paying close attention to the scale of inflows that India’s capital flow measures would attract over ‌a period to bolster India’s FX kitty that is used to defend the rupee.

On Tuesday, ‌the Indian currency rose 0.2% to 96.2350 against ‌the ⁠US dollar.
